sentences.text: The Jews from part of that ghetto were the street Rudnicka, I remember.
And I can't remember all the names.
But Rudnicka was the entrance to the ghetto.
All the Jews who lived there were taken to Ponary, the mass graves, and killed.
So there was empty.
So now they had a space to rest of the people to take in the ghetto.
And we walked.
And I remember I had a broomstick.
So one side, my baby was holding on, on the other side my mother.
And I was holding the stick.
And that's how we walked to the ghetto.
By the entrance to the ghetto, there were people laying there people already, because there was no food, starvation, and beatings, and all the things.
And it was an absolute disaster.
It was a hell no human being can visualize this.
And then we went in the ghetto.
And we had to look for a place to stay, because the ghetto was already filled up.
And we were going from one house to another, three floors, four floors, to walk up.
And we knocked, and opened up, and said, it's filled up.
We used to put 40, 50 people in one apartment.
Finally, this was in the morning when we started.
It was already getting late.
It was like 5:00, 6:00 o'clock in the evening.
And my baby was starving, and my mother.
And there was no water.
And I came, finally, to a second, third floor apartment somewhere there.
And I knocked.
And the door opened.
And here was standing a man.
And he said like that, we are filled up, but come in anyway.
And that's how we found a place to go in.
And there was about 40 people already in this apartment.
I remember there was a living room.
There was a bedroom, and a little kitchen.
And we were standing by the wall.
At night, we had no way to sit down or lay down.
But I made place from my mother.
I was next to the door.
So I was standing by the wall.
And she, my mother, stretched out next to my feet.
And my baby, and I was standing all night long.
Like, that's how we-- that was a night, you know?
But it started-- this was-- this was like before the Jewish holidays, like a month or something.
And then it started again, the selection, the raids on the ghetto, and taking people to death.
And at night, 3:00 or 4:00 o'clock at night, you used to hear the screaming, because Nazis, with the help from the Poles, with the Lithuanians, all help, let me tell you.
There were some good people.
But, you know, there were a few.
The rest were cooperating with the Nazis.
And I remember from-- like I don't know how many, 40 people, or 50 people in that apartment.
But time the raids was over, after the holidays, was left like 20 people, 18 people.
They were all taken already out.
Every week, they would come.
And they say, this.
They'd point out who is going to go.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then my-- my family were all gone, you know?
My oldest sister, and-- three-- two sisters were in [NON-ENGLISH].
And then my mother went there too, because they moved us from-- there was-- we called it little ghetto, the big ghetto.
And we were in the big ghetto.
Then they moved me to another part of the ghetto, another apartment, [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't remember.
And we were like three families now in one room, in one room.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I start talking.
And I can't talk.
It hurts too much.
In the-- yeah, I worked.
OK.
My baby, I put up with a lot of children.
There was a day school for little kids.
And I got a job-- I want to remember-- yeah, my sister, before the-- before the Nazis-- before the Russians, the communists came in, had a neighbor, Mr.
Bagdonas.
He had a wife, and three or four children, three or four girls, I think.
And when the Russian communists came in, they were trying to get all the-- Lithuanian, because he was in the ministry.
He was a big man, you know?
And they were all going-- making rides at night, and arrested them, send them to Siberia, or killed them.
So my sister was hiding this family, because they were next door neighbors, in her apartment.
At night, they used to put them in-- in the day they had like a garage or something.
So my sister saved the life of Mr.
Bagdonas, his wife, and his children.
So when the Nazis came in, Bagdonas became a big shot.
He worked.
He was the chef of police for the Nazis, for the Germans on [NON-ENGLISH], the street [NON-ENGLISH].

And he was giving-- he gave my brother-in-law that name, a [NON-ENGLISH], a document to put where you can walk the street, because the Jews weren't allowed to walk on the street, only on the roads, on the roads, with animals.
So he had that.
And he was trying to help them.
And then-- I want to remember.
And he had a brother who was the head-- a brother-in-law, excuse me, his wife's brother, who was the head of meat transport-- not-- how do you call this?
Exporting and importing meats.
He was the big man, you know?
And he came to the ghetto.
And he found me.
And he said to me that he wants to help me, that his brother Mr.
Bagdonas.
What I can do is to-- that I can do some sewing, repairing clothes for his family.
So I will have food.
They will give me-- can't bring nothing in the ghetto, because they were searching you before entering.
But used to hide in the pants somewhere where they wouldn't search you, because I had nothing to eat anymore.
What they gave you the food, is starvation.
There was nothing, a slice of bread for three days or something.
And my baby-- so I put my baby in together with this group of children, in a room for children.
And every morning, I would go out to work with a group of people which went to that [NON-ENGLISH], street [NON-ENGLISH], Great [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], where that Mr.
Bagdonas lived with his family.
And I came there.
And they gave me breakfast to eat.
And I used to sit all day long and do repair clothes for his wife and children.
And then when I left, they used to give me some bread from my baby.
So I used to hide it somewhere, a little piece, because they were searching you.
But they couldn't take too much out.
And that went on for a while, until in that-- across from this Bagdonas, lived a woman who was a little crippled on her leg, a Polish woman.
And one day, she saw me coming in, and going out from-- going to where the group of the people to meet.
And she reported me to this Lithuanian police.
And when I came out from Mr.
Bagdonas house, walking back to meet the group, the working group, to go back to the ghetto, two policeman, Lithuanian, they were in charge, picked me up.
And they took me to the police station in [NON-ENGLISH].
There was the headquarters there also.
And I didn't know why they did that.
And they said, you are Jewish.
In ghetto, you're not supposed to get-- do you have a [NON-ENGLISH]?
And I didn't have no [NON-ENGLISH].
And the men were sitting-- there was a man sitting in the room, you know?
He took me in.
And I told them that I worked for Mr.
Bagdonas.
And he didn't believe me.
He said, you're a Jew.
And you lie.
And he said, you're going to go to Lukiskes, which the people were sent to Ponary to death.
So I ask him a favor, if you could get in touch with Mr.
Bagdonas.
He says, no, Jew, you a Jew, so and so, you know?
And I waited from the hole that the police had to pick me to take me to Ponary, to Lukiskes Ponary.
But the girl from the ghetto, from [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish girl used to clean their place there, what is meant to be alive, huh?
She saw me.
And she knew who I was.
I don't know, because she probably knew me from [NON-ENGLISH], where I used to come and maybe visit my sister or something.
So she had mentioned my sister's name, probably.
That's what it was.
And she-- and one, she ran from that place.
She walked, ran, to get to [NON-ENGLISH].
She got there, and told my sister, my brother-in-law, that I am in prison to take to Lukiskes to death.
Could you believe that?
And I'm sitting, and waiting.
And all of a sudden, I hear the telephone in the big man's office, you know?
And on telephone, then he comes out me.
And he says, Mrs.
Ingster, Mr.
Bagdonas.
And Bagdonas was the head of the head.
He was a big man.
He was, like, second.
He said, Mr.
Bagdonas told me to let you go free to the ghetto.
And I called the ghetto.
And they will come.
They sent the policeman from ghetto to pick you up, and take you back to the ghetto.
And that girl was running barefoot all the way so far to get my brother-in-law.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sure enough, I'm-- excuse me.
And now, two hours later, two policeman from ghetto came, and took me back to the ghetto.
What else do you want to know?
I forgot you ask me, because since my stroke, I don't remember.
Your last name at that time was Ingster also?
Yeah, Ignster was my family name.
My maiden name was Bastocka.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, I know, wait, wait, I messed up.
This was my second.
My first, Ejszyszok was my-- my-- sorry.
Oh, I would try many time.
But the problem is I couldn't leave my baby.
You know the famous singer [NON-ENGLISH], they were my neighbors before the war, where my mother lived.
You know, she was the famous singer in-- opera singer, not in Germany, in-- oh, God, what's the next-- not Switzerland.
What was the next country?
Anyway, she sang in all the operas all over the world.
And she was-- she was-- in ghetto-- she came into the ghetto, you know, because she shouldn't have been.
She was living abroad.
But she came to visit her my mother and her brother.
And they caught her in the ghetto.
And I remember, once I met her where they were checking us out by the ghetto.
And they arrested her, because she had a piece of bread.
They found break, and took her to Lukiskes and Ponary killed her for a piece of bread.
[NON-ENGLISH], very famous opera singer, in Vienna.
She lived in Vienna.
She was very famous opera singer, [NON-ENGLISH].
And I met her by the entrance to ghetto.
They arrested her because they found a piece of bread, and took her to Lukiskes and then to Ponary and killed her.
In what?
Ponary?
They know that people were killed, sure.
From 1941 June, till before the liquidation, 1943, when they took me.
They said they were going to take me to Estonia in the transport.
I don't know, September probably.
I have something written down.
I will give it to you.
This was just when it was announced on the walls in the ghetto that the ghetto was going to be liquidated.
And there was no hope.
We know we are all going to die.
OK, and everybody was kissing and hugging.
I kissed my baby, and she me.
And we know that's end.

And then there was another announcement came just a week before the liquidation, because there was already a lot of children put on the trucks, taken to death, that anybody who has a relative in Estonia, they will pick up the people on the trucks, and take them to the cattle train to Estonia.
And I had my father-in-law in Estonia.
I knew that, because I know when they picked him up.
He was the only one still alive from the whole family.
So I went to the Jewish community there off [NON-ENGLISH] Street.
And I said, my father-in-law is in Estonia.
And I would like to go to my father-in-law.
So that's when, within a few days, my baby and me was taken on the transport to Estonia.
And for-- first, they took us somewhere for a couple-- we thought that they are lying.
They're taking us to kill us in Ponary, you know, because we were standing for three nights somewhere.
I don't know where we were.
And then we started finally to move.
And after 10 days, we came to Estonia.
Is
All liquidated, all killed.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They killed almost all people in the ghetto, in Vilna ghetto, very a few who came out alive.
The Partisan who escaped in the woods, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was some people who escaped, the young man, and some woman, in the woods.
And some survived.
Some were killed when they were caught.
But there's not too many people from Vilna ghetto.
There are some here in the States, because every year we ever used to get together when I lived in New York.
The memorial, you know, for the Vilna ghetto was in New York in a synagogue.
I don't remember where it was.
Oh, I could have gotten out, and maybe run in the street.
I had Christian friends.
But there was monastery close to the ghetto.
And I could have-- some, a couple of Jewish women went in the monastery.
Some, they were hiding.
Some, they gave it back to the police.
Would you believe it?
Because they were afraid, probably.
I don't know.
But I guess I could have tried.
The problem was I wouldn't leave my baby under no circumstances.
Tell me about being transported out.
How were you transported?
Can you describe it for me?
On truck, we waited outside the ghetto.
And there came these trucks, you know?
And they took it in.
And they took us to this station somewhere where there were these cattle trains, with the wire little windows.
And that's where.
Right.
Pardon?
Not this one.
This one stopped the first two nights, three nights.
I don't know where we were.
We thought we were going to Ponary to be shot.
But then, finally, we went to Estonia.
We stopped in-- the first stop was-- Vivikond.
I don't-- no Vaivara.
And from there, they took us to Vivikond.
We walked, I think.
I can't remember how it was.
Klooga?
Klooga, Klooga.
Klooga, this was going to Auschwitz from Estonia.
We stopped there, because there was a concentration camp.
I wrote it down.
But they didn't let us out.
They took out only the dead body.
We never heard from Auschwitz.
I never knew.
Otherwise, I would have been the gas chambers.
But--
It was nothing there.
It was like the [NON-ENGLISH] in German.
A round barracks, it's not wood.
It's made of some kind of-- what do you call it, what you bend like?
It's not a canvas.
It was not a metal.
It's like a paper, which you-- which is bent.
It was half round.
There was no floors.
That was just-- I can draw it for you, if you want to.
And-- and that the camp in Estonia.
That's how this-- except the last one, in Vivikond, Vivikond or Ereda, I don't-- I think Vivikond had regular buildings, these camp structures.
This, we slept on the plain earth, you know?
There was nothing.
And they liquidated within seven weeks, this Vivikond.
And they put us to Ereda.
We walked to Ereda.
And from Ereda to-- to-- no, to-- to Vaivara.
And from Vaivara to Ereda.
Let's stay in Vivikond for a minute.
Who were your guards?
Guards were German SS and Estonian.
Yeah, yeah.
I kept mine baby in Vivikond, I don't remember, with me.
No, she was in another barrack.
But the doctor there from Vilna who knew me, was a friend.
And I used to-- because they wouldn't let us in to see the children.
So he used to-- he was in charge, because the children was sick.
So I used to go in, see my baby, you know?
And then- and then in Vaivara, it was only seven weeks we were.
They then liquidated Vivikond.
And they shot a lot of people who-- by liquidating it, you know, I wanted to go with my kid.
And they said, no, you will go separate.
And the children will walk separate.
We walked, you know?
So they start shooting everybody, like that.
And so I was walking.
But then, finally, the halfway through, somehow I stole through.
I will have seen it-- and got my baby with me, you know?
But we came to Vaivara.
And there was a barracks where all they kept the children separate.
But you know who was-- Ejszyszok was in Vivikond, in Vaivara, and in Ereda.
He was the head commandant, that murderer.
And I was the only one who could testify, [INAUDIBLE] from Israel.
My daughter went to the gas chambers was five.
So she was four years old, probably. 1943, so she was four years old.
Etele, I don't know how to say-- Ida, like in for grownups would be Ida.
But Etele in Yiddish Hebrew.
How could you help each other?
You were tortured.
You were beaten.
You worked on the railroad.
That's why I have a broken toe on my left foot, from-- because we were working on the railroad.
And the rail-- you know, the long-- fall on my foot here.
And it was swollen.
I was in-- in [NON-ENGLISH], in rags, because I couldn't even walk straight.
So there was no food.
There was zero food.
They made soup from earth, from brown earth, and some kind of chemicals.
They didn't even have bread, OK?
In Estonia, it was nothing to eat.
It's starvation.
When I escaped the first time with my baby from the transport, which went to Auschwitz, and I-- this was later on.
When they liquidate all the children, and I begged [NON-ENGLISH] from Helmut Schnabel to let me keep my baby.
And he says, no, all children have to go, and all the one who was sick with typhoid, OK?
Finally, kissing, and asking him, and praying, he let me.
He said, OK, go, because he knew it was going to death.
I said, where are we going?
He said, we go to [NON-ENGLISH], to Klooga, to all the other camps, to some camp.
We came to that camp.
But we didn't taken out.
Only the dead people taken out.
And there was no food in the camp.
There was nothing to eat.
People were starving.
We're eating-- find pieces of raw potatoes, or something.
In all the camp, there was nothing to eat.
Even Auschwitz, the only food we got was from the people that went to the gas chambers, and the food what they had with them in the train.
That's what they used to give for the people there.
They had no food.
As far as I know, we're all Jewish, yeah.
All over Poland.
No.
Killings and executions, oh, Helmut Schnabel had a couple of helpers with him.
And every day, he walked around like a wild dog animal, looking what to do.
So I saw-- he didn't know that I say.
But I used to say, he used to go in, and take that big gun, and [SHOOTING].
And a lot of the people, the men-- he used to kill mostly men.
He had a hatred toward men.
And the helpers used to cut the men.
And first, they used to beat them, and break their legs.
You know, they hear the screaming, and the things what was going on, you know?
And he was-- every day, he was executing people.
The only non-Jews which I had was in Auschwitz.
They were from France, and from Belgium, and from Poland and from-- During your time in Estonia, what was happening with the children that were in the camp?
They had them in a barrack.
And one of the one who watches them was a teacher that I remember from childrens.
Sarah was her name.
She had two children of her own.
And she was watching over the kids.
And it was zero food.
I don't know.
I don't remember had any pieces of bread.
I remember I once saw a couple of these Christian who work for the Nazis, pulling a cart with some bread.
And I ran over.
And I grabbed a piece of bread.
And they hit me over the head.
But I grabbed it anyway.
I didn't give it back.
And I hide right away, because-- we were in deep snow.
Estonia was-- the winter was horrifying.
You know, it was-- when we came there-- I don't remember what day it was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the beginning, it was like September, October.
But in November, they were all in snow covered.
So I was hiding in the snow behind that big entrance, behind the guard.
And then I gave-- I had my baby, you know?
I kept her until-- you see, when they took the baby, separated the children in another barrack, I couldn't let this happen.
This was in Ereda.
So at night, I came to take out my baby from the barrack.
And that woman said, I said, you can't do it, she says.
You know what will happen when they find out?
I said, don't tell me what I can do.
This is my child, because most of the children had typhoid.
And my daughter had typhoid in Vaivara, in the barracks.
And when she got better, I had typhoid, because I caught it from her, typhoid fever.
And we had no medicine, nothing, naturally.
And the children in Ereda were all already sick with typhoid there.
And she said, I can't have it.
I says, oh, yes, I says, I can.
And I take my baby.
And I took my baby.
It was at night.
It was pitch dark.
And I know the SS guard was standing and guarding me.
But, nevertheless, I found a way.
I crawled through the snow and the tanks.
And I went into my barrack.
And the woman there in my barrack said, what did you do?
I said, don't say nothing.
That's my baby.
She's going to stay in my bed, you know?
And I kept my baby until the-- before the liquidation, when somebody went and told Schnabel that there's a baby hiding.
Would you believe that?
And that's when I was forced to go with my baby, and that we went to the death, you know?
But I had her hidden.
Vaivara, she get typhoid.
And she was with the children.
And I used to-- and they didn't let the parents in.
But I was in anyway.
I just crawled in.
And I stayed with her.
She didn't know.
She was unconscious, because she had such high temperature.
And we had no medicine, just plain water.
That's all.
But I was lucky to get her water.
The other kids was starving, they didn't even have water.
And they had nobody to take care of them.
So I was the only one crazy mother who wasn't afraid for the guns and from the Nazis.
And I just crawl in after the appel.
You know, when they have the appel, everybody goes to work.
And I-- and they took me to work, in the beginning, to dig potatoes from the-- they were hiding, a potato company, you know?
But then when I found out that my daughter is ill, every night, I used to crawl in there and see her.
I didn't go back anymore.
And I said to the girls, and I said, you can report me, whatever you want.
I'm staying, because my baby is here, sick.
So I used to go in in the morning after the appel, when they count you, and they send people to work.
And I crawled in somewhere to the barrack through the door.
And I was staying next to my baby, and giving her water, because she was unconscious.
The temperature was so high.
If she would have died then, she wouldn't have had to go to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the gas chambers, you know?
But I didn't know.
I wanted to save my child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I'm sorry.
I just can't talk about it.
We're going to take a break now and change the tape.
It's OK.
